Mai mulţi oficiali americani au confirmat că analizează opţiuni pentru o posibilă invazie a Cubei, luând drept model invazia anuală pe care România o desfăşoară în insula Thasos. „Faptul că luăm în calcul un asemenea scenariu nu înseamnă neapărat că preşedintele Trump e […] The post SUA fac planuri să invadeze Cuba după modelul României, care invadează Thasosul în fiecare an appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Anchetă la Cluj, după ce o clinică stomatologică din Turcia ar fi anunțat consultații într-un hotel din oraș. Ce spune firma acuzată
romania.summary: Este anchetă în Cluj-Napoca, după ce o clinică stomatologică din Turcia ar fi anunțat că va face consultații într-un hotel din oraș. Colegiul Medicilor Dentiști a sesizat Poliția că spațiul nu este nici pregătit, nici autorizat pentru astfel de consultații. În replică, reprezentanții clinicii dau asigurări că nu vor avea loc consultații medicale. Evenimentul s-ar adresa doar celor care au beneficiat, în trecut, de serviciile lor.

romania.title: 😂 Dragnea îşi regretă alegerea: Mai bine păstram ştiuca la PSD şi îl aruncam pe Grindeanu înapoi în baltă
romania.summary: Când ai câteva minute la dispoziţie să alegi între două creaturi acvatice similar de alunecoase, n-ai de unde să ştii care e varianta corectă! Ajuns la vârsta înţelepciunii, fostul şef PSD Liviu Dragnea spune că, privind în urmă, i se pare că a făcut alegerea greşită. „Eram acolo, pe barcă, eu, Grindeanu şi ştiuca, dar […] The post Dragnea îşi regretă alegerea: Mai bine păstram ştiuca la PSD şi îl aruncam pe Grindeanu înapoi în baltă appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: Sweeteners slow growth of important gut bacteria in lab tests
medical.summary: Cambridge researchers have shown how commonly used sweeteners slow the growth of certain gut bacteria. One sweetener in particular—isosteviol—when combined with the antidepressant duloxetine, significantly impaired two important gut bacteria linked to blood sugar regulation and gut health and may affect the body's immune responses.

medical.title: Researchers estimate the upper limit of human lifespan determined by somatic mutations
medical.summary: Researchers from Skoltech and co-authors from AIRI have, for the first time, quantitatively assessed how somatic mutations—random DNA changes that accumulate in cells with age—limit the maximum human lifespan. The work was published in the journal npj Aging.

medical.title: What's driving the obesity pandemic?
medical.summary: New research presented at the International Congress on Obesity (ICO2026), hosted by the World Obesity Federation (WOF) in Mexico City, Mexico, (15–17 July) asks what is driving the obesity pandemic.

medical.title: Research confirms depression impacts all 5 senses
medical.summary: A new study examining the impact of depression on all five senses could significantly improve how the condition is diagnosed and treated, opening the door to more targeted solutions for patients. Led by the University of New England's Professor of Neuroscience, Christopher Sharpley, alongside colleagues Dr. Lorenzo Odierna, Professor Vicki Bitsika, and Christopher Watson, the first-of-its-kind study highlights how depression is more than just a cognitive-emotional disorder.

medical.title: A hospital that pays for itself? Sounds like a fairy tale
medical.summary: Once upon a time, in Anywhere, U.S., there's a place called "Fable Hospital" where patients heal faster, staff stay longer and the building is designed to work with—not against—the people inside it. It's not a real place, but it was designed to solve a very real problem: why hospitals can be expensive to run, hard on staff and not always built for the best patient care.

medical.title: Scientists tame seizures and heal injured brains with gut chemical
medical.summary: The skull heals its cracks, the bruises fade, the swelling subsides and the bleeding stops. From the outside, a traumatic blow to the head, whether from a battlefield blast, car crash or even a tackle gone wrong, may seem like it's healed. But that's only the opening act of traumatic brain injury (TBI).

medical.title: How a farming programme in Sierra Leone helps amputees rebuild lives
medical.summary: An empowering initiative teaches people with limb loss how to farm sustainably, enabling many to start businesses The post How a farming programme in Sierra Leone helps amputees rebuild lives appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Home visits could cut hospital trips by a third
medical.summary: Getting patients seen by community health teams within a day of leaving the hospital could slash emergency readmissions and emergency department visits, a new study by University of Manchester researchers suggests.

medical.title: Scientists discover alternative B-cell development pathway in birds
medical.summary: Birds possess a specialized organ called the bursa of Fabricius that mammals do not have. It has long been thought that B cells, part of the immune system, develop exclusively in this organ. However, researchers from Tohoku University have discovered a previously unknown pathway for B-cell development in chickens, overturning the long-standing belief.

medical.title: What went right this week: eco leader Estonia, plus more
medical.summary: The world’s ‘greatest forest recovery’, a new test for womb cancer, and Europe’s environmental progress, plus more The post What went right this week: eco leader Estonia, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: Hidden feeding grounds that fuel one of the ocean's most iconic sportfish identified
science.summary: Atlantic tarpon are famous for their long-distance migrations, traveling hundreds or even thousands of miles (hundreds to thousands of kilometers) along the Gulf and Atlantic coasts. But where they feed along those journeys has remained largely unknown until now.

science.title: Rethinking urban nature as technonature
science.summary: A new Comment article by Simon Marvin, Sophia Maalsen and Robyn Dowling published in the journal Nature Cities argues that urban nature is entering a new phase in which ecological processes are increasingly sensed, modeled and continuously adjusted through digital technologies.

science.title: What does it mean to be 'quantum?' A physicist explains the basics behind Einstein's spooky actions at a distance
science.summary: Imagine shining a flashlight across a dark room. You can predict exactly what the light will do: travel in a straight line from one point to another. That seems obvious because, in the world we see around us, light appears to follow a single, clear path.

science.title: Helpful microbes could battle pathogens in our hospitals and schools—with the help of AI to make it work
science.summary: Helpful microbes that combat harmful pathogens could be the answer to rising antimicrobial resistance—particularly within built environments such as hospitals, homes and schools.

science.title: Epigenetic mechanism explains how some plants cope with salt stress
science.summary: Due to artificial irrigation and rising temperatures, the concentration of salts (including sodium chloride, or "table salt") is increasing in soils worldwide. This is not only an environmental problem but also a challenge for agriculture. For example, increased salinity can disrupt the water balance of most plants (known as glycophytes) or even lead to their death, including crops.

science.title: After traveling a billion kilometers, China's asteroid hunter finally arrives
science.summary: What does it take to catch up with a small, tumbling rock hundreds of thousands of kilometers from Earth? For China's Tianwen-2 mission, the answer was a 400-day chase covering roughly 1 billion kilometers (621 million miles) of deep space—one that has just ended in success. The China National Space Administration has confirmed that the probe has rendezvoused with the near-Earth asteroid Kamoʻoalewa, also known as 2016 HO3, closing to within about 20 kilometers (12 miles) and officially beginning its scientific exploration phase.

science.title: Marsupial newborns get early arms as embryos bypass usual limb-building sequence
science.summary: Scientists have discovered that marsupial forelimbs (arms) develop much earlier before birth than previously thought, providing new insights into evolutionary innovation and biology.

science.title: NASA Study of Pristine Meteorite Adds to Story of Ancient Asteroids
science.summary: A meteorite recovered immediately upon its fall to Earth on July 16, 2024, is helping NASA scientists uncover new clues about ancient water, the chemical evolution of primitive asteroids, and the ingredients that may have helped make life possible throughout the early solar system.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Sweeteners slow growth of important gut bacteria in lab tests
mix_items.summary: Cambridge researchers have shown how commonly used sweeteners slow the growth of certain gut bacteria. One sweetener in particular—isosteviol—when combined with the antidepressant duloxetine, significantly impaired two important gut bacteria linked to blood sugar regulation and gut health and may affect the body's immune responses.

mix_items.title: Researchers estimate the upper limit of human lifespan determined by somatic mutations
mix_items.summary: Researchers from Skoltech and co-authors from AIRI have, for the first time, quantitatively assessed how somatic mutations—random DNA changes that accumulate in cells with age—limit the maximum human lifespan. The work was published in the journal npj Aging.

mix_items.title: What's driving the obesity pandemic?
mix_items.summary: New research presented at the International Congress on Obesity (ICO2026), hosted by the World Obesity Federation (WOF) in Mexico City, Mexico, (15–17 July) asks what is driving the obesity pandemic.

mix_items.title: Research confirms depression impacts all 5 senses
mix_items.summary: A new study examining the impact of depression on all five senses could significantly improve how the condition is diagnosed and treated, opening the door to more targeted solutions for patients. Led by the University of New England's Professor of Neuroscience, Christopher Sharpley, alongside colleagues Dr. Lorenzo Odierna, Professor Vicki Bitsika, and Christopher Watson, the first-of-its-kind study highlights how depression is more than just a cognitive-emotional disorder.

mix_items.title: A hospital that pays for itself? Sounds like a fairy tale
mix_items.summary: Once upon a time, in Anywhere, U.S., there's a place called "Fable Hospital" where patients heal faster, staff stay longer and the building is designed to work with—not against—the people inside it. It's not a real place, but it was designed to solve a very real problem: why hospitals can be expensive to run, hard on staff and not always built for the best patient care.

mix_items.title: Scientists tame seizures and heal injured brains with gut chemical
mix_items.summary: The skull heals its cracks, the bruises fade, the swelling subsides and the bleeding stops. From the outside, a traumatic blow to the head, whether from a battlefield blast, car crash or even a tackle gone wrong, may seem like it's healed. But that's only the opening act of traumatic brain injury (TBI).

mix_items.title: How a farming programme in Sierra Leone helps amputees rebuild lives
mix_items.summary: An empowering initiative teaches people with limb loss how to farm sustainably, enabling many to start businesses The post How a farming programme in Sierra Leone helps amputees rebuild lives appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Home visits could cut hospital trips by a third
mix_items.summary: Getting patients seen by community health teams within a day of leaving the hospital could slash emergency readmissions and emergency department visits, a new study by University of Manchester researchers suggests.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover alternative B-cell development pathway in birds
mix_items.summary: Birds possess a specialized organ called the bursa of Fabricius that mammals do not have. It has long been thought that B cells, part of the immune system, develop exclusively in this organ. However, researchers from Tohoku University have discovered a previously unknown pathway for B-cell development in chickens, overturning the long-standing belief.

mix_items.title: Hidden feeding grounds that fuel one of the ocean's most iconic sportfish identified
mix_items.summary: Atlantic tarpon are famous for their long-distance migrations, traveling hundreds or even thousands of miles (hundreds to thousands of kilometers) along the Gulf and Atlantic coasts. But where they feed along those journeys has remained largely unknown until now.

mix_items.title: Rethinking urban nature as technonature
mix_items.summary: A new Comment article by Simon Marvin, Sophia Maalsen and Robyn Dowling published in the journal Nature Cities argues that urban nature is entering a new phase in which ecological processes are increasingly sensed, modeled and continuously adjusted through digital technologies.

mix_items.title: What does it mean to be 'quantum?' A physicist explains the basics behind Einstein's spooky actions at a distance
mix_items.summary: Imagine shining a flashlight across a dark room. You can predict exactly what the light will do: travel in a straight line from one point to another. That seems obvious because, in the world we see around us, light appears to follow a single, clear path.

mix_items.title: Helpful microbes could battle pathogens in our hospitals and schools—with the help of AI to make it work
mix_items.summary: Helpful microbes that combat harmful pathogens could be the answer to rising antimicrobial resistance—particularly within built environments such as hospitals, homes and schools.

mix_items.title: Epigenetic mechanism explains how some plants cope with salt stress
mix_items.summary: Due to artificial irrigation and rising temperatures, the concentration of salts (including sodium chloride, or "table salt") is increasing in soils worldwide. This is not only an environmental problem but also a challenge for agriculture. For example, increased salinity can disrupt the water balance of most plants (known as glycophytes) or even lead to their death, including crops.

mix_items.title: After traveling a billion kilometers, China's asteroid hunter finally arrives
mix_items.summary: What does it take to catch up with a small, tumbling rock hundreds of thousands of kilometers from Earth? For China's Tianwen-2 mission, the answer was a 400-day chase covering roughly 1 billion kilometers (621 million miles) of deep space—one that has just ended in success. The China National Space Administration has confirmed that the probe has rendezvoused with the near-Earth asteroid Kamoʻoalewa, also known as 2016 HO3, closing to within about 20 kilometers (12 miles) and officially beginning its scientific exploration phase.

mix_items.title: Marsupial newborns get early arms as embryos bypass usual limb-building sequence
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered that marsupial forelimbs (arms) develop much earlier before birth than previously thought, providing new insights into evolutionary innovation and biology.

mix_items.title: NASA Study of Pristine Meteorite Adds to Story of Ancient Asteroids
mix_items.summary: A meteorite recovered immediately upon its fall to Earth on July 16, 2024, is helping NASA scientists uncover new clues about ancient water, the chemical evolution of primitive asteroids, and the ingredients that may have helped make life possible throughout the early solar system.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: What went right this week: eco leader Estonia, plus more
mix_items.summary: The world’s ‘greatest forest recovery’, a new test for womb cancer, and Europe’s environmental progress, plus more The post What went right this week: eco leader Estonia, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Am deschis frigiderul doar de trei ori. Progres emotional.

satire_ro.title: SUA fac planuri să invadeze Cuba după modelul României, care invadează Thasosul în fiecare an
